era_context: The Old Kingdom of Egypt (Dynasty VI) continues its gradual decline. Naram-Sin's Akkad Empire remains at its height. The Harappan civilization is in its mature phase. This period represents the apex of Bronze Age civilization in the Old World, though internal stresses and climate variability were beginning to accumulate.

events.title: Pepi II's reign: administrative fragmentation in Egypt
events.description: During Pepi II's reign, the power of provincial governors (nomarchs) continued to grow at the expense of the central pharaonic authority. Inscriptions from this period show nomarchs conducting independent military campaigns and accumulating wealth and land. This decentralization was a hallmark of the late Old Kingdom and would accelerate into the First Intermediate Period.
events.certainty_note: No specific events are recorded for this year. The characterization is based on the broader pattern of Pepi II's reign and the late Old Kingdom.

events.title: Naram-Sin's reign: apex of Akkad Empire
events.description: Naram-Sin's reign represented the height of Akkadian imperial power. He controlled territories from the Persian Gulf to the Mediterranean and from the Taurus Mountains to the Arabian Peninsula. His administration was sophisticated, with a centralized bureaucracy and standardized administrative practices. However, internal resistance and external pressure from the Gutians were building.

sources.name: Akkadian royal inscriptions and administrative texts

events.certainty_note: Naram-Sin's reign is well-attested, but no specific events are recorded for this particular year.

events.title: Harappan urban centers at peak development
events.description: The major Harappan urban centers (Mohenjo-daro, Harappa, Dholavira, Kalibangan) were fully developed and functioning at their peak. These cities featured sophisticated urban planning with grid-pattern streets, standardized brick sizes, advanced drainage systems, and public buildings. The civilization's economy was based on agriculture, craft production, and long-distance trade.

sources.name: Archaeological excavations and surveys of Indus Valley sites

events.certainty_note: The Harappan civilization's existence and chronology are well-established archaeologically. The mature phase is dated c. 2600–1900 BCE.

disconfirming_evidence: No specific events are recorded for this year. The entries are generalizations about broader patterns.
historiographic_note: This year is undocumented in the historical record. The entries represent inferences from archaeological and epigraphic evidence for the broader period.

graph_edges.note: Administrative fragmentation during Pepi II's reign contributed to the Old Kingdom's collapse
